applicationDefinition: A conversational interface that answers common library questions and points users to the right services, policies, or resources. It can handle routine requests around hours, borrowing, and events while escalating more complex questions to staff. In practice, it works best when paired with a knowledge base and clear guardrails.
libraryExample: A public library chatbot answers questions about hours, borrowing policies, and upcoming programs.

applicationDefinition: An AI tool that helps users shape a research question, find relevant terms, and identify promising sources or search strategies. It can support early-stage exploration, but staff or the user still need to verify source quality and relevance. It is most useful as a guided starting point rather than a replacement for expert research support.
libraryExample: A university library research assistant helps a student build search terms for a literature review.

applicationDefinition: A tool that helps users create, format, or check citations in a chosen style. It can reduce formatting errors and save time when moving between styles such as APA, MLA, or Chicago. Final review is still important because source details and style rules can be easy to misapply.
libraryExample: A student pastes in source information and gets help converting citations into APA format.

applicationDefinition: A system that condenses long articles, reports, or books into shorter versions that preserve the main ideas and findings. It can help users scan large amounts of material quickly and decide what deserves a full read. Summaries should be checked against the original source for nuance, omissions, and accuracy.
libraryExample: A graduate student uses summaries to decide which journal articles to read in full.

applicationDefinition: A language-support tool that helps staff and patrons communicate across languages. It can translate basic questions, directions, and service information so more users can access support in their preferred language. Human review matters when the exchange involves policy, nuanced reference questions, or sensitive topics.
libraryExample: A librarian uses translation support to assist a patron who prefers Spanish.

applicationDefinition: A search approach that retrieves results by meaning and intent instead of exact keyword matches. It can surface useful materials even when the user does not know the right vocabulary, which is especially helpful for interdisciplinary or exploratory searches. Because similarity is probabilistic, results still need curation and transparency.
libraryExample: A search for climate impacts on farming returns items about drought, agriculture, and food security.

applicationDefinition: A recommendation system that suggests resources based on user behavior, interests, or usage patterns. In a library setting, it can surface books, media, or services a user might otherwise miss. The design should be careful about privacy, bias, and the possibility of narrowing rather than broadening discovery.
libraryExample: A reader who borrows mystery novels gets recommendations for new crime fiction titles.

applicationDefinition: A search feature that adds related terms, synonyms, or broader subjects to improve retrieval. It can help bridge wording differences between users and catalog language, especially when a topic has multiple names or spellings. Users should be able to see and control the expanded query when possible.
libraryExample: A search for 'heart attack' suggests related medical terms such as myocardial infarction.

applicationDefinition: A reading recommendation tool that suggests books or media and explains how they match a user's interests. It can mimic the role of a librarian by connecting appeal factors, genres, and themes to likely matches. Because generative systems can overstate fit, staff or user review is still useful.
libraryExample: A patron asks for read-alikes to a favorite fantasy novel and receives suggested titles with brief appeal notes.

applicationDefinition: A cataloging support tool that suggests subject headings, descriptors, or keywords from item content. It can speed up first-pass metadata work and help surface terms that staff might want to review or refine. Outputs should be checked for consistency, local practice, and coverage across communities.
libraryExample: A cataloger reviews subject heading suggestions for a newly acquired book.

applicationDefinition: A tool that creates draft descriptions, abstracts, keywords, or other metadata for staff review. It is useful for speeding up routine descriptive work or for generating a starting point when many items need treatment quickly. Human review is essential to verify accuracy, controlled vocabulary, and local cataloging standards.
libraryExample: A repository generates a draft description for a faculty publication before staff review it.

applicationDefinition: A quality-control tool that identifies inconsistent, incomplete, or potentially incorrect metadata records. It can flag missing values, mismatched fields, duplicate variants, or records that need authority control. The goal is to accelerate review, not to replace cataloger judgment.
libraryExample: A library uses AI to flag records with missing publication dates and inconsistent subject terms.

applicationDefinition: A text-analysis tool that identifies names, places, organizations, dates, and other entities in documents. In libraries and archives, it can help turn unstructured text into searchable, linked, or normalized metadata. Results are usually strongest when paired with human validation and local name authority practices.
libraryExample: An archive extracts names and locations from oral history transcripts.

applicationDefinition: A matching tool that identifies likely duplicate records so staff can merge or resolve them. It can help clean shared catalogs, repository records, or imported metadata at scale. Because false matches can create serious damage, the tool should be used with conservative thresholds and review workflows.
libraryExample: A shared catalog flags multiple records that appear to describe the same edition.

applicationDefinition: A digitization tool that converts scanned images of text into machine-readable text. It makes historical newspapers, books, and documents searchable and more accessible, especially when paired with clean output and page-level structure. Accuracy can vary with image quality, fonts, and layout complexity.
libraryExample: A library digitizes historical newspapers and makes them searchable by keyword.

applicationDefinition: A vision-based tool that converts handwritten text into machine-readable text. It can help open up correspondence, field notes, marginalia, and archival materials that are otherwise difficult to search. Since handwriting is highly variable, staff review and correction are usually required.
libraryExample: An archive transcribes handwritten letters so researchers can search them digitally.

applicationDefinition: A computer vision tool that identifies objects, people, places, or themes in images. Libraries can use it to speed up description, tagging, and discovery for photograph or visual media collections. The system should be audited for bias, mislabeling, and uneven performance across image types.
libraryExample: A photograph collection is automatically tagged with subject terms and locations.

applicationDefinition: A speech technology tool that converts recorded audio or video into written text. It supports access, searching, and downstream editing for interviews, lectures, and oral histories. Accuracy depends on audio quality, accents, background noise, and domain-specific vocabulary.
libraryExample: A library creates transcripts for oral history interviews to improve access and searchability.

applicationDefinition: A generative tool that creates draft descriptions for items in digital collections or archives. It can reduce the time needed to produce basic access points for large sets of material. Staff should review the output for descriptive accuracy, bias, and representation.
libraryExample: A digital exhibit receives draft descriptions for photographs before staff review them.

applicationDefinition: An analytics process that identifies subjects, communities, or perspectives that may be underrepresented in a collection. It can help selectors compare holdings against community needs, curricular priorities, or diversity goals. The results are only as good as the data and criteria used to define the gap.
libraryExample: A public library checks whether Indigenous authors are adequately represented in its holdings.

applicationDefinition: A prediction tool that estimates future demand for materials, services, or formats. It can help libraries plan purchases, staffing, and access models around expected use patterns. Forecasts should be treated as planning inputs, not fixed outcomes, because user behavior and context can change.
libraryExample: A library predicts increased demand for tax-preparation materials during the spring.

applicationDefinition: A decision-support tool that suggests materials for purchase based on needs, trends, or usage patterns. It can help selectors scan a larger market and prioritize items that align with local goals. Recommendations should remain suggestions rather than automatic decisions, especially in niche or underserved areas.
libraryExample: A selector receives recommended titles in an emerging research area.

applicationDefinition: A review tool that flags materials that may deserve closer evaluation for retention, storage, or withdrawal. It can surface low-use items, duplicates, or aging content to help staff focus their review time. Final decisions should account for collection policy, historical value, and local context.
libraryExample: A library generates a list of low-use items for staff review before making weeding decisions.

applicationDefinition: An analytics process that examines circulation, database use, website activity, or service metrics. It helps libraries understand what is being used, where demand is rising, and where services may need adjustment. Privacy protections and careful interpretation are essential.
libraryExample: A library reviews database usage data before renewing a subscription.

applicationDefinition: A data-analysis approach that uses historical patterns to forecast future outcomes or needs. Libraries may use it for staffing, space planning, service demand, or collection decisions. Predictions should be reviewed for bias and validated against real-world conditions.
libraryExample: A library predicts future study room demand based on prior usage trends.

applicationDefinition: An interactive learning tool that teaches research skills, source evaluation, and information literacy concepts. It can provide practice questions, explanations, and step-by-step guidance tailored to the learner's needs. The tutor should reinforce good research habits rather than just supply answers.
libraryExample: A student gets guided practice evaluating whether a source is credible.

applicationDefinition: A translation tool that converts text or speech between languages to support communication and access. It can help users navigate the library, understand instructions, or participate in services without waiting for human translation. The output still needs review when precision matters.
libraryExample: A patron uses a service desk kiosk that translates catalog instructions into their preferred language.

applicationDefinition: A productivity tool that creates summaries, action items, and notes from meetings or discussions. It helps staff quickly capture decisions and next steps without manually transcribing long conversations. Sensitive meetings require careful handling because summarizers may omit nuance or expose private information.
libraryExample: A library committee receives an automatically generated summary after a planning meeting.

applicationDefinition: A writing assistant that helps draft, refine, or organize grant proposals. It can speed up early drafting, tighten language, and help structure complex sections such as goals, methods, or impact. Users still need to verify funder requirements, originality, and factual claims.
libraryExample: A researcher uses AI to outline a grant narrative and strengthen the language in a proposal.

applicationDefinition: An analysis tool that visualizes themes, connections, or trends across a body of research. It can help researchers identify clusters, influential works, and underexplored topics more quickly than manual reading alone. The maps are most useful when combined with subject expertise and source verification.
libraryExample: A librarian maps the main topics appearing in recent articles on digital humanities.

applicationDefinition: An analytics process that examines citations, altmetrics, or collaboration patterns to estimate research influence. Libraries can use it to support faculty reporting, promotion dossiers, or institutional assessment. Metrics should be interpreted carefully because they do not capture every dimension of scholarly value.
libraryExample: A library helps a faculty member review citation trends for a promotion dossier.

applicationDefinition: A tool that helps researchers draft plans for organizing, preserving, and sharing research data. It can prompt users to think through storage, documentation, access, and retention before a project begins. A human review is still important to ensure the plan matches funder and institutional requirements.
libraryExample: A graduate student uses AI to outline a data management plan for a grant application.

applicationDefinition: A curation tool that creates thematic reading lists based on topics, events, courses, or interests. It can help staff assemble a useful starting point for programs, instruction, or outreach faster than manual searching alone. Human review helps keep selections balanced, current, and audience-appropriate.
libraryExample: A librarian generates a reading list for a community discussion on climate resilience.

applicationDefinition: A natural-language search tool that lets users ask questions instead of building keyword searches. It can translate plain-language requests into relevant catalog results and explain why items were returned. Good design should make the retrieval logic visible enough for users to trust and refine the search.
libraryExample: A patron asks, "Which beginner gardening books cover container planting?" and receives catalog results with short explanations.

applicationDefinition: An AI system that can plan and carry out multi-step research tasks with limited human intervention. It may search, synthesize, compare sources, and iterate on the user's behalf, which makes it powerful but also risky. Strong guardrails, logging, and human oversight are necessary because errors can compound across steps.
libraryExample: A graduate student asks the system to identify major themes in recent AI-in-education literature.
